title: OAuth 2.0 — Refresh Token Grant
description: Access tokens are intentionally short-lived (minutes to hours). When one expires, the client uses its refresh token to obtain a new access token from the Authorization Server — without requiring the user to re-authenticate. This flow covers the full lifecycle: detecting a 401, silently refreshing, retrying the request, refresh token rotation (a security best practice), and what happens when the refresh token itself expires or is replayed after rotation.

steps.label: GET /api/profile (expired token → 401)

response_headers.WWW-Authenticate: Bearer realm="api.example.com", error="invalid_token", error_description="The access token expired"

artifacts.signing_key_hint: AS's ES256 key — signature valid, but exp has passed

steps.label: Why the token is rejected
steps.value:
Access token payload:
{
  "iss": "https://auth.example.com",
  "sub": "user_alice_482",
  "aud": "https://api.example.com",
  "exp": 1699999000,   ← expired at 2023-11-14T23:50:00Z
  "iat": 1699995400,
  "jti": "old-at-123"
}

RS validation sequence:
  1. Fetch AS public key from JWKS → signature OK ✓
  2. iss matches expected AS ✓
  3. aud matches this RS ✓
  4. exp check: 1699999000 < now (1700002600) → EXPIRED ✗

The RS returns 401 with WWW-Authenticate indicating
error=invalid_token. The client must refresh.

Access tokens are kept short-lived (minutes to a few hours)
deliberately. If a token is stolen, it quickly becomes useless.
OAuth Security Best Current Practice (RFC 9700) recommends
keeping access token lifetimes as short as feasible.

steps.explanation: The client makes an API request with an access token that has expired. The resource server validates the JWT, confirms the signature and claims are otherwise valid, but rejects the request because exp < now. It returns a 401 with WWW-Authenticate indicating error=invalid_token.
annotations: RFC 6750 §3.1: The RS MUST respond with HTTP 401 and a WWW-Authenticate header including error=invalid_token when the access token has expired.
The client should check exp before sending requests (clock skew: subtract ~30 seconds) to avoid making a request that will fail. But it must also handle 401 for unexpected expirations.
Short-lived access tokens are a security feature. A stolen token has a limited window of usefulness. The refresh token enables transparent renewal without user friction.
The client should not reveal to the user that the token expired — it should silently refresh and retry the original request.

steps.label: POST /token (refresh_token grant)

artifacts.signing_key_hint: AS validates refresh token and applies rotation policy
artifacts.final_output: New access token + new (rotated) refresh token. Old refresh token invalidated.

steps.label: 1. AS validates the refresh token
steps.value:
The AS checks the submitted refresh token:
  • Is it in the valid token store? (not revoked, not expired)
  • Does it belong to client_id=s6BhdRkqt3? (binding check)
  • Is the client authentication valid? (Basic auth header)
  • Has the user's authorization been revoked?

RFC 6749 §6: The AS MUST verify the refresh token was issued
to the authenticated client. A refresh token presented by a
different client MUST be rejected.

steps.label: 2. Refresh token rotation (security best practice)
steps.value:
Rotation policy: issue a NEW refresh token, invalidate the OLD one.

  old: tGzv3JOkF0XG5Qx2TlKWIA  ← invalidated immediately
  new: 8xLOxBtZp8Ry7q4mNbKWIA  ← issued, stored by client

Why rotation matters:
  If an attacker steals a refresh token and uses it, the
  legitimate client's NEXT refresh attempt will fail with
  invalid_grant (the old RT is gone). This tells the
  legitimate client that something is wrong.

  If the AS detects a refresh token from an already-rotated
  family ("RT family invalidation" / "refresh token reuse"),
  it SHOULD revoke the ENTIRE token family for that user+client
  pair — a sign of token theft. See step 4.

RFC 9700 Best Current Practice §2.2.2: Rotation is strongly
recommended. Sender-constrained refresh tokens (e.g., DPoP-
bound) provide additional protection if the token is leaked.

steps.label: 3. What the AS can change on refresh
steps.value:
On issuing new tokens, the AS:
  • MUST NOT expand the scope beyond what was originally granted
  • MAY issue a shorter-lived access token (step-down)
  • MAY choose NOT to rotate the refresh token (less secure)
  • MAY change the access token's claims (e.g., updated roles)
  • MUST check that the user's authorization is still valid
    (the user may have revoked consent since the original grant)

Scope downscoping: The client MAY request a subset of the
original scope: grant_type=refresh_token&refresh_token=...&scope=read:profile
(omitting write:reports). The AS MUST NOT grant more than the original.

steps.explanation: The client silently exchanges its refresh token for a new access token. The AS issues a fresh access token and a rotated refresh token (the old refresh token is immediately invalidated). The user sees none of this — from their perspective, the app just keeps working.
annotations: RFC 6749 §6: grant_type=refresh_token. The refresh_token is the only required body parameter (besides client authentication).
The scope in the new access token MUST NOT exceed the originally granted scope. The client MAY request a narrower scope.

Refresh tokens should be stored securely (encrypted at rest). If a public client (SPA, native app) cannot securely store secrets, use short-lived access tokens + silent re-authorization instead of refresh tokens.
RFC 9700 §2.2.2: Sender-constrained refresh tokens (DPoP or mTLS bound) prevent stolen refresh tokens from being used by an attacker even if the storage is compromised.

steps.label: GET /api/profile (retry with new token → 200)
steps.explanation: The client retries the original API request with the new access token. The resource server validates the fresh token (exp is in the future) and returns the protected resource. The user experienced no interruption.
annotations: The entire refresh cycle (steps 1–3) is transparent to the user — they see only a momentary delay (typically < 200ms) before the app continues normally.
Implementations should implement a proactive refresh: if exp - now < threshold (e.g., 30 seconds), refresh before making the API call rather than waiting for a 401.
The new access token has a fresh jti (new-at-456) — the RS sees it as a brand new token, not a renewal of the old one.

steps.label: POST /token (old RT replayed → family revocation)

response_body.error_description: Refresh token has already been used (token reuse detected). All sessions for this client have been revoked.

artifacts.signing_key_hint: AS detects reuse of a previously rotated refresh token
artifacts.final_output: error=invalid_grant + full family revocation (both old and new refresh tokens invalidated)

steps.label: Refresh token family invalidation (theft detection)
steps.value:
Scenario: The original refresh token (tGzv3JOkF0XG5Qx2TlKWIA)
was stolen before step 2. The attacker uses it AFTER
the legitimate client already rotated it in step 2.

Timeline:
  t=0  : refresh_token=tGzv3...issued (auth code flow)
  t=1h : legitimate client refreshes → rotated to 8xLOx...
         old token tGzv3... INVALIDATED
  t=1h+: ATTACKER presents tGzv3... (the old, rotated token)

AS sees:
  tGzv3... is in the "used/rotated" state (not valid, not fresh)

  This is a REUSE event — the old token was already rotated.
  Someone has a copy of it.

AS response (RFC 9700 §2.2.2):
  • Reject the current request with invalid_grant
  • Revoke the ENTIRE token family:
      - tGzv3...  (old, already invalidated)
      - 8xLOx...  (new, currently valid) ← also revoked
  • Both the attacker and legitimate client are now logged out

Why revoke the whole family?
  Because we don’t know WHO used the token first.
  Either the attacker used it before the client (meaning
  the client’s 8xLOx... was also obtained by the attacker),
  OR the client used it first and the attacker is using
  the stolen old token. In either case, the session is
  compromised — safest action is full revocation.

The legitimate user will be required to re-authenticate.

steps.explanation: An attacker (or a misconfigured retry) attempts to use the already-rotated refresh token. The AS detects this reuse, rejects the request, and revokes the ENTIRE token family — including the currently valid refresh token issued in step 2. Both parties are logged out. The user must re-authenticate.
annotations: RFC 9700 §2.2.2: If token reuse is detected on a rotated token, the AS SHOULD revoke all tokens in the family to mitigate the impact of token theft.
This mechanism is what makes rotation effective as a theft detection tool, not just an expiry mechanism.
The legitimate user should be notified that their session was terminated due to a suspicious event — this is a security signal worth surfacing.
Sender-constrained refresh tokens (DPoP or mTLS) prevent this class of attack: even if the attacker has the token value, they cannot use it without the bound private key.
